passage.title: The Secret Life of Soil
passage.text:
Beneath your feet lies a hidden world that is teeming with life. It may look like plain dirt, but soil is one of the most important and complex ecosystems on the planet. Without healthy soil, there would be no forests, no farms, and no food on our tables.

Soil is made up of several layers. The top layer, called topsoil, is the darkest and richest part. It contains decomposed plant and animal matter known as humus, which gives soil its dark colour and provides nutrients for plants. Below this lies subsoil, which is lighter in colour and contains more clay and minerals. Deeper still is the bedrock — the solid rock foundation that, over millions of years, breaks down to form new soil.

What makes soil truly remarkable is the incredible number of organisms that live within it. A single handful of healthy soil can contain more living creatures than there are people on Earth. These include bacteria, fungi, tiny worms called nematodes, and insects such as springtails and beetle larvae. Earthworms are perhaps the most well-known soil dwellers. As they tunnel through the ground, they break down dead leaves and mix organic matter into the soil. Charles Darwin, the famous scientist, spent decades studying earthworms and concluded that they were among the most important creatures in the history of the world.

Fungi also play a crucial role. Many plants form partnerships with underground fungi called mycorrhizae. The fungi attach to plant roots and extend thread-like structures called hyphae far through the soil. These hyphae act like tiny straws, drawing in water and nutrients that the plant's roots alone could not reach. In return, the plant shares sugars with the fungi. Scientists have discovered that trees in a forest can even share nutrients with each other through these fungal networks. Some researchers call this the "wood wide web."

Unfortunately, soil is under threat in many parts of the world. Clearing land for farming can strip away the topsoil, a process called erosion. Using too many chemical fertilisers can kill the helpful organisms that keep soil healthy. It takes nature roughly five hundred years to produce just two centimetres of topsoil, so once it is lost, it cannot be quickly replaced.

Protecting soil means protecting everything that depends on it — from the tiniest bacterium to the tallest tree, and from the farmer's crop to the food on your plate. Healthy soil is not just dirt; it is the foundation of life.

questions.question: What is this passage mainly about?
options: Why earthworms are the most important animal on Earth
The layers, living organisms, and importance of soil
How to grow vegetables in your backyard garden
The life and work of Charles Darwin
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is B because the passage covers soil's structure (layers), the organisms that live in it (bacteria, fungi, earthworms), and why it matters (supporting all life). It provides a comprehensive overview of soil as an ecosystem.

Option A is incorrect because earthworms are discussed in one paragraph as an example, not as the sole focus of the passage.

Common mistake: Students might choose A because Darwin's quote about earthworms is memorable, but the passage covers many topics beyond earthworms.

questions.question: What is humus?
options: A type of underground fungus
The solid rock layer at the bottom of soil
Decomposed plant and animal matter in topsoil
A chemical fertiliser used by farmers
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is C because the passage states humus is 'decomposed plant and animal matter' that 'gives soil its dark colour and provides nutrients for plants.' It is found in the topsoil layer.

Option B is incorrect because the solid rock foundation is called bedrock, not humus.

Common mistake: Students might confuse humus with other soil components. The passage clearly places humus in the topsoil and defines it as decomposed organic matter.

questions.question: What does the word 'teeming' mean in 'a hidden world that is teeming with life'?
options: Completely empty
Full of and overflowing with
Slowly running out of
Quietly hiding
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is B because 'teeming' means swarming or full of something in large numbers. The passage goes on to describe the enormous number of organisms in soil, supporting this meaning.

Option A is incorrect because 'teeming' is the opposite of empty — it means there is an abundance of life.

Common mistake: Students unfamiliar with the word might guess from context. The passage immediately describes the many organisms in soil, confirming that 'teeming' means full of life.

questions.question: How do mycorrhizal fungi help plants?
options: They protect plants from insects that eat their leaves
They draw in water and nutrients that plant roots cannot reach on their own
They produce oxygen for the plant to breathe
They block other plants from growing too close
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is B because the passage explains that fungi 'extend thread-like structures called hyphae far through the soil. These hyphae act like tiny straws, drawing in water and nutrients that the plant's roots alone could not reach.'

Option A is incorrect because the passage does not mention fungi protecting plants from insects.

Common mistake: Students might choose A because protection is a common benefit in nature, but the passage specifically describes the fungi's role in extending the plant's nutrient-gathering reach.

questions.question: Why do some scientists call the fungal network the 'wood wide web'?
options: Because it was discovered using the internet
Because trees use it to connect and share resources, like a network
Because the fungi look like spider webs under the ground
Because the network only works in wooden structures
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is B because the passage explains that 'trees in a forest can even share nutrients with each other through these fungal networks.' The name 'wood wide web' is a play on 'World Wide Web' (the internet) because both are networks that connect and share resources — one connects computers, the other connects trees.

Option C is incorrect because while the hyphae are thread-like, the name refers to the network's function of sharing, not its physical appearance.

Common mistake: Students might choose C because 'web' makes them think of spider webs and the hyphae are thread-like, but the name is about the network's function of connecting and sharing.

questions.question: What is the relationship between plants and mycorrhizal fungi?
options: The fungi harm the plants by stealing their nutrients
The plants and fungi help each other — the fungi provide nutrients and the plants provide sugars
The plants control the fungi and tell them where to grow
The fungi only help the plants during winter
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is B because the passage describes a mutualistic relationship: the fungi 'drawing in water and nutrients' for the plant, and 'In return, the plant shares sugars with the fungi.' Both organisms benefit from the partnership.

Option A is incorrect because the relationship is mutually beneficial — the fungi help the plant, and the plant provides sugars in return.

Common mistake: Students might think one organism must dominate the other, but the passage clearly describes a two-way exchange where both benefit.

questions.question: How is the passage organised?
options: It tells a story about a scientist who discovered something about soil
It describes soil's structure, then its living organisms, then threats to it
It lists steps for how to create healthy soil at home
It compares soil in Australia with soil in other countries
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is B because the passage moves through clear sections: first the layers of soil (topsoil, subsoil, bedrock), then the organisms living in it (bacteria, fungi, earthworms), and finally the threats to soil (erosion, chemicals). This is a logical, topic-by-topic organisation.

Option C is incorrect because the passage does not provide instructions — it explains and informs.

Common mistake: Students might choose A because Charles Darwin is mentioned, but he appears in one sentence as a supporting detail, not as the subject of a story.

questions.question: What does the author suggest would happen if topsoil is lost?
options: It can easily be replaced within a few months
Farmers can use chemicals to create new soil quickly
It would take hundreds of years to replace naturally
New topsoil would form from the bedrock within a year
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is C because the passage states 'It takes nature roughly five hundred years to produce just two centimetres of topsoil, so once it is lost, it cannot be quickly replaced.' This strongly implies that losing topsoil is extremely serious because replacement takes centuries.

Option A is incorrect because the passage says the exact opposite — topsoil takes roughly 500 years to form.

Common mistake: Students might choose B because chemical fertilisers are mentioned, but the passage presents chemicals as a threat to soil, not a solution for rebuilding it.

questions.question: Why does the author include the statistic about a handful of soil containing more creatures than people on Earth?
options: To warn readers that soil is dangerous
To help readers understand just how many organisms live in soil
To compare soil creatures with human beings
To prove that soil is the same everywhere in the world
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is B because the author uses this dramatic comparison to help readers grasp the enormous number of living things in soil. By comparing it to something readers can relate to (the world's human population), the author makes the invisible world of soil feel real and impressive.

Option C is incorrect because the comparison is not about likeness between creatures and humans — it is about scale and quantity.

Common mistake: Students might choose C because humans are mentioned, but the comparison is a technique to illustrate scale, not to draw similarities between organisms and people.

questions.question: What is the author's main message in the final paragraph?
options: Soil is just dirt and is not very interesting
Farmers are the only people who need to care about soil
Healthy soil is essential for all life and must be protected
The best way to protect soil is to stop farming completely
questions.workedSolution:
The correct answer is C because the final paragraph says 'Protecting soil means protecting everything that depends on it' and concludes with 'Healthy soil is not just dirt; it is the foundation of life.' The author emphasises that soil supports all living things and needs protection.

Option B is incorrect because the passage shows that soil supports everything from bacteria to trees to our food — it is not just a farming issue.

Common mistake: Students might choose D because the passage mentions farming as a threat, but the author does not suggest stopping farming — the message is about protecting soil through better practices.
